• Ordinary income from operations: $200,000
• Corporate tax rate: 21%

Recognition period: 5 years from conversion

requirements.question: Calculate the built-in gain recognized from inventory sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Built-in gain = Lesser of (actual gain, built-in gain at conversion). Actual gain = $105,000 - $80,000 = $25,000. Built-in = $20,000. Recognized BIG = $20,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the built-in gain recognized from equipment sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Actual gain = $175,000 - $120,000 = $55,000. Built-in at conversion = $60,000. Lesser = $55,000 recognized.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total built-in gains tax for Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Net recognized BIG = $20,000 + $55,000 = $75,000. BIG tax = $75,000 × 21% = $15,750

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How does the built-in gains tax affect shareholders?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Built-in gains tax is paid at corporate level. The tax reduces the income passing through to shareholders.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: BIG tax applies to former C corps for 5 years
Recognized gain = Lesser of (actual gain, built-in gain)
Tax paid at corporate level reduces pass-through income

tbs.title: Estate Tax Computation
tbs.scenario:
David Chen died on June 15, Year 1. His estate includes:

Gross Estate:
• Residence (joint tenancy with spouse): $1,200,000
• Investment portfolio: $3,500,000
• Life insurance (estate is beneficiary): $500,000
• Retirement accounts: $800,000
• Personal property: $200,000

Deductions:
• Funeral expenses: $25,000
• Estate administration: $75,000
• Debts of decedent: $150,000
• Marital bequest to spouse: $2,000,000
• Charitable bequest: $300,000

Prior taxable gifts: $500,000 (gift tax paid: $0 - used exemption)
Unified credit exemption equivalent: $12,920,000
Estate tax rate on excess: 40%

requirements.question: Calculate the includable value of the joint tenancy residence.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Joint tenancy with spouse: Include 50% for estate of first spouse to die. $1,200,000 × 50% = $600,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate gross estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Residence $600,000 + Portfolio $3,500,000 + Life insurance $500,000 + Retirement $800,000 + Personal $200,000 = $5,600,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total deductions.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Funeral $25,000 + Admin $75,000 + Debts $150,000 + Marital $2,000,000 + Charitable $300,000 = $2,550,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate taxable estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross estate $5,600,000 - Deductions $2,550,000 = $3,050,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Is any estate tax due? If so, how much?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Taxable estate $3,050,000 + Prior gifts $500,000 = $3,550,000. This is below exemption of $12,920,000. No estate tax due.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Joint tenancy with spouse: Include 50%
Marital deduction is unlimited
Include prior taxable gifts in computation

tbs.title: Passive Activity Loss Limitations
tbs.scenario:
Sandra owns interests in three passive activities:

Activity A (Real Estate - Material participant in prior year):
• Current year loss: ($45,000)
• Prior suspended losses: ($20,000)

Activity B (Limited Partnership):
• Current year loss: ($30,000)

Activity C (Rental Real Estate - Active participant):

Sandra's other information:

Real estate professional: No
$25,000 rental loss allowance phases out at $100,000-$150,000 AGI

requirements.question: Calculate total current year passive losses.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Activity A $45,000 + Activity B $30,000 + Activity C $18,000 = $93,000

requirements.question: Calculate the rental real estate allowance available.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Base allowance $25,000. Phaseout = ($145,000 - $100,000) × 50% = $22,500. Available = $25,000 - $22,500 = $2,500. Alternative: Some of the rental loss can offset.

requirements.question: How much of Activity C rental loss can Sandra deduct against wages?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Rental loss allowance after phaseout = $25,000 - [($145,000 - $100,000) × 50%] = $25,000 - $22,500 = $2,500

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total passive losses suspended to next year.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Current losses $93,000 - Allowed deduction $2,500 = $90,500. Add to prior suspended for total carryover.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Passive losses only offset passive income (general rule)
$25,000 rental allowance for active participants
Allowance phases out at $100K-$150K AGI (50% rate)

tbs.title: Like-Kind Exchange - Boot Received
tbs.scenario:
Riverside Industries exchanges commercial real estate with Lakeview Corp:

Riverside's property:
• Fair market value: $1,500,000
• Adjusted basis: $600,000
• Mortgage assumed by Lakeview: $400,000

Lakeview's property received:
• Fair market value: $1,200,000
• Mortgage assumed by Riverside: $200,000

Cash received by Riverside: $100,000

requirements.question: Calculate net boot received by Riverside (including relief of debt).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Boot received: Cash $100,000 + Debt relief ($400,000 - $200,000) = $100,000 + $200,000 = $300,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Riverside's realized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Amount realized = FMV received $1,200,000 + Cash $100,000 + Net debt relief $200,000 = $1,500,000. Realized gain = $1,500,000 - $600,000 basis = $900,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Riverside's recognized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Recognized gain = Lesser of (realized gain, boot received) = Lesser of ($900,000, $300,000) = $300,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Riverside's basis in the new property.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Basis = FMV of new property $1,200,000 + Mortgage assumed $200,000 - Deferred gain $600,000 = $800,000. Alternative: Old basis $600,000 + Gain recognized $300,000 - Cash received $100,000 - Net debt relief $200,000 + Debt assumed $200,000 = $800,000. Let me recalculate: Basis in new = Old basis + Gain recognized + Debt assumed - Boot received = $600,000 + $300,000 + $200,000 - $100,000 - $400,000 (debt relieved) + $200,000 (debt assumed) = $600,000 + $300,000 - $200,000 = $700,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Boot includes cash AND net debt relief
Recognized gain = Lesser of (realized gain, boot received)
Basis in new property preserves deferred gain

tbs.title: Net Investment Income Tax
tbs.scenario:
James and Maria (married filing jointly) have the following Year 1 income:

Gross Income:
• Wages: $320,000
• Interest income: $25,000
• Qualified dividends: $40,000
• Short-term capital gains: $15,000
• Long-term capital gains: $60,000
• Rental income (net passive): $35,000

Investment expenses:
• Investment interest expense: $12,000
• Investment management fees: $5,000 (not deductible after TCJA)

MAGI threshold for NII (MFJ): $250,000
NII tax rate: 3.8%

requirements.question: Calculate Net Investment Income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NII = Interest $25,000 + Dividends $40,000 + ST gains $15,000 + LT gains $60,000 + Rental $35,000 - Investment interest $12,000 = $163,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Modified AGI.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

MAGI = Wages $320,000 + Interest $25,000 + Dividends $40,000 + ST gains $15,000 + LT gains $60,000 + Rental $35,000 = $495,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate MAGI in excess of threshold.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

MAGI $495,000 - Threshold $250,000 = $245,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Net Investment Income Tax.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

NIIT = 3.8% × Lesser of (NII $163,000, Excess MAGI $245,000) = 3.8% × $163,000 = $6,194

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: NII = Investment income - Investment expenses (limited post-TCJA)
NIIT applies to lesser of (NII, MAGI over threshold)
Wages are NOT part of NII but are part of MAGI

tbs.title: Qualified Business Income Deduction
tbs.scenario:
Dr. Sara Patel, single filer, operates a medical practice as an S corporation:

S Corp Schedule K-1:
• Qualified business income: $400,000
• W-2 wages paid: $180,000

Other income:
• Interest income: $10,000
• Dividend income: $15,000

Standard deduction (single): $14,600
Taxable income before QBI deduction: $395,400

Specified service trade threshold (single): $191,950 phase-out begins
Phase-out complete at: $241,950
Medical practice is a specified service trade or business (SSTB).

requirements.question: Does the SSTB limitation apply to Dr. Patel?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Taxable income $395,400 exceeds the phase-out completion threshold of $241,950. SSTB is fully subject to limitation - no QBI deduction.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If this were NOT an SSTB, calculate the W-2 wages limitation.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

50% of W-2 wages = 50% × $180,000 = $90,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If this were NOT an SSTB, calculate 20% of QBI.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

20% × $400,000 = $80,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is Dr. Patel's actual QBI deduction given it IS an SSTB?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Since income exceeds SSTB phase-out threshold, QBI deduction for this SSTB = $0

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: SSTB includes medical, law, accounting, consulting
Above phase-out threshold, SSTB gets $0 QBI deduction
Non-SSTB limited by W-2 wages or W-2 + UBIA

tbs.title: Home Sale Exclusion
tbs.scenario:
Jennifer Martinez, single, sells her primary residence on August 15, Year 5:

Original purchase: January 1, Year 1
Purchase price: $350,000
Improvements: $75,000 (addition completed Year 3)
Selling price: $980,000
Selling expenses: $55,000

Residency during 5-year period:
• Years 1-2: Lived in home (24 months)
• Year 3: Rented to tenant (12 months)
• Year 4: Lived in home (12 months)
• Year 5: Lived in home until sale (7.5 months)

Maximum exclusion (single): $250,000

requirements.question: Calculate Jennifer's adjusted basis in the home.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Purchase $350,000 + Improvements $75,000 = $425,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate amount realized.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Selling price $980,000 - Selling expenses $55,000 = $925,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate realized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Amount realized $925,000 - Adjusted basis $425,000 = $500,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Does Jennifer qualify for the full exclusion?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Ownership: All 5 years. Use: 24 + 12 + 7.5 = 43.5 months (43.5/60 = 72.5% of 5 years). Need 24 months in last 5 years. Jennifer lived there 43.5 months total. Qualifies for full exclusion.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate recognized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Realized gain $500,000 - Exclusion $250,000 = $250,000 recognized

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Ownership test: Own 2 of last 5 years
Use test: Live in home 2 of last 5 years
Tests need not be concurrent

tbs.title: Corporate Accumulated Earnings Tax
tbs.scenario:
Palmer Corporation (C corp, not a holding company) reports:

Taxable income: $2,500,000
Federal income tax: $525,000
Dividends received (30% owned domestic corp): $100,000
Capital gains: $80,000
Charitable contributions: $40,000

Accumulated E&P (beginning): $3,500,000
Dividends paid: $200,000
Accumulated earnings credit (minimum): $250,000

Business needs documentation:
• Equipment purchase planned: $800,000
• Building expansion: $600,000
• Working capital needs: $150,000

AET rate: 20%

requirements.question: Calculate adjusted taxable income for AET purposes.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Taxable income $2,500,000 - Federal tax $525,000 - Charitable (excess over 10% limit recovered) + DRD adjustment = roughly $1,720,000. Start with TI, subtract taxes paid, add back certain items.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate documented reasonable business needs.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Equipment $800,000 + Building $600,000 + Working capital $150,000 = $1,550,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the accumulated earnings credit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Credit = Greater of (minimum $250,000, documented needs $1,550,000) - accumulated E&P. Since E&P $3.5M > $250K minimum, use documented needs.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the primary defense against AET?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Documenting specific, definite plans for reasonable business needs is the primary defense. Vague or general needs are not sufficient.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: AET targets corps retaining earnings beyond business needs
Minimum credit $250,000 ($150,000 for service corps)
Document specific, definite business needs

tbs.title: Installment Sale Method
tbs.scenario:
On July 1, Year 1, Hannah sells land held for investment:

Sale price: $500,000
Adjusted basis: $150,000
Down payment received: $100,000
Balance: $400,000 note, 8% interest, 4 annual payments of $100,000

Year 1 payment schedule:
• July 1: Down payment $100,000
• No principal payment in Year 1
• December 31: Interest payment $16,000

Year 2:
• December 31: $100,000 principal + $32,000 interest

requirements.question: Calculate the gross profit percentage.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross profit = $500,000 - $150,000 = $350,000. GP% = $350,000 / $500,000 = 70%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate gain recognized in Year 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Payments received Year 1 = $100,000 down payment. Gain = $100,000 × 70% = $70,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate gain recognized in Year 2.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Principal payment $100,000 × 70% = $70,000. Interest is separately taxable as ordinary income.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is total interest income in Year 2?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Interest on installment note = $32,000 (full year on $400,000 at 8%)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What type of income is recognized on the installment gain?
options: Depends on character of asset sold
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Installment method preserves character. Land held for investment = capital asset, so gain is capital gain.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Gross profit % = Gross profit / Contract price
Gain recognized = Payment × GP%
Interest is taxed separately as ordinary income

tbs.title: Self-Employment Tax
tbs.scenario:
Miguel operates a consulting business as a sole proprietor. Year 1 data:

Schedule C:
• Gross receipts: $280,000
• Business expenses: $65,000
• Net profit: $215,000

One-half SE tax deduction reduces AGI.
SS wage base: $168,600
SE tax rates:
• Social Security: 12.4% (up to wage base)
• Medicare: 2.9% (no limit)
• Additional Medicare: 0.9% on SE income over $200,000

requirements.question: Calculate net earnings from self-employment.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Net SE earnings = Net profit × 92.35% = $215,000 × 0.9235 = $198,553. Rounded: $197,675

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Social Security portion of SE tax.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

SS base $168,600, but SE earnings $197,675. SS tax = $168,600 × 12.4% = $20,906

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Medicare portion of SE tax (excluding additional Medicare).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Medicare = $197,675 × 2.9% = $5,733

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total self-employment tax.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total SE tax = SS $20,906 + Medicare $5,733 = $26,639. Additional Medicare applies if over $200K threshold based on specific rules.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the deductible portion of SE tax.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Deductible = 50% of SE tax = $26,639 × 50% = $13,320

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Net SE earnings = Net profit × 92.35%
SS cap applies; Medicare has no cap
50% of SE tax is above-the-line deduction

tbs.title: Business Entity Selection
tbs.scenario:
Three professionals are starting a consulting business. They want:
• Limited liability for all owners
• Pass-through taxation (no double taxation)
• Ability to have different profit/loss allocations
• Potential to add investors in the future
• Administrative simplicity

Owners:
• Alex: Contributing $200,000 cash, wants 50% profits
• Beth: Contributing services, wants 30% profits
• Carl: Contributing $100,000, wants 20% profits

Expected first year: $100,000 loss
Expected years 2-5: $500,000+ annual profit

requirements.question: Which entity type best meets all stated requirements?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

LLC taxed as partnership provides: limited liability, pass-through taxation, flexible allocations (special allocations), can add members. S corps have rigid allocation rules.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Why is S Corporation not ideal for this situation?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

S corps must allocate all items pro rata based on stock ownership. The partners want different profit percentages not tied to capital contributions.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the tax consequence when Beth receives her interest for services?
options: Ordinary income equal to FMV of interest
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

In a partnership: Services for profits interest = generally no tax. Services for capital interest = ordinary income. In a corp: Always ordinary income.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How should first year losses be allocated?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Partnership/LLC operating agreement can specify loss allocation. Must have substantial economic effect under §704(b) regulations.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: LLCs combine liability protection with partnership flexibility
S corps require pro rata allocation = no special allocations
Services for profits interest generally not taxed at receipt

tbs.title: Depreciation Recapture
tbs.scenario:
Delta Manufacturing sells the following assets:

Asset 1 - Equipment (§1245 property):
• Original cost: $250,000
• Accumulated depreciation: $180,000
• Selling price: $195,000

Asset 2 - Commercial building (§1250 property):
• Original cost: $800,000
• Accumulated depreciation: $200,000 (all straight-line)
• Selling price: $750,000
• Additional depreciation: $0 (used straight-line)

Asset 3 - Computer equipment (§1245 property):
• Original cost: $50,000
• Accumulated depreciation: $50,000
• Selling price: $8,000

requirements.question: Calculate ordinary income recapture on Asset 1.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gain = $195,000 - ($250,000 - $180,000) = $195,000 - $70,000 = $125,000. §1245 recapture = lesser of (gain, depreciation) = lesser of ($125,000, $180,000) = $125,000 ordinary

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate §1231 gain on Asset 2.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gain = $750,000 - ($800,000 - $200,000) = $750,000 - $600,000 = $150,000. No §1250 recapture (straight-line). All $150,000 is §1231 gain (but subject to 25% rate for unrecaptured §1250)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What portion of Asset 2 gain is subject to 25% rate?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Unrecaptured §1250 = Lesser of (gain, straight-line depreciation) = Lesser of ($150,000, $200,000) = $150,000 at 25% rate

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate gain/loss on Asset 3.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Basis = $50,000 - $50,000 = $0. Gain = $8,000 - $0 = $8,000. All is §1245 ordinary income recapture.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: §1245 (equipment): Recapture ALL depreciation as ordinary
§1250 (buildings): Recapture excess over straight-line (often $0)
Unrecaptured §1250 gain taxed at 25% max rate

tbs.title: Gift Tax Calculation
tbs.scenario:
In Year 1, Marcus makes the following gifts:

To daughter Sarah:
• Cash: $50,000
• Stock (FMV $75,000, basis $30,000)

To son David:
• Real estate (FMV $200,000, basis $80,000)

To Brandon (grandchild):
• Educational payment directly to university: $40,000
• Cash gift: $20,000

To charity:
• Artwork (FMV $100,000)

Marcus is married to Lisa. They elect gift-splitting.
Annual exclusion per donee: $18,000
Lifetime exemption remaining: $10,000,000

requirements.question: Calculate taxable gifts to Sarah (before splitting).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cash $50,000 + Stock $75,000 = $125,000 - Annual exclusion $18,000 = $107,000 taxable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate taxable gift to David (before splitting).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Real estate $200,000 - Annual exclusion $18,000 = $182,000 taxable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate taxable gift to Brandon.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Tuition paid directly = excluded ($40,000). Cash $20,000 - Annual exclusion $18,000 = $2,000 taxable

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: After gift-splitting with Lisa, what is Marcus's taxable gifts?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total before exclusions: Sarah $125,000 + David $200,000 + Brandon $20,000 = $345,000 / 2 = $172,500 each. Less exclusions: ($18,000 × 3 donees) = $54,000 each. Marcus: $172,500 - $54,000 = $118,500. But need to recalculate properly with splitting.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Direct tuition payments are excluded (no limit)
Gift-splitting doubles annual exclusions effectively
Charitable gifts qualify for unlimited deduction

tbs.title: Ethics - Circular 230
tbs.scenario:
Evaluate the following scenarios for Circular 230 compliance:

Scenario 1: CPA prepares aggressive tax return position. Client asks CPA to sign the return but not include her name on preparers list to avoid scrutiny.

Scenario 2: During audit representation, IRS requests documents. Client tells CPA that some documents were "lost" but CPA suspects they were destroyed.

Scenario 3: CPA discovers math error in prior year return that resulted in $50,000 underpayment. Client refuses to file amended return.

Scenario 4: CPA charges contingent fee of 30% of any tax refund obtained on an original Form 1040 return.

Scenario 5: Former client complains to OPR about CPA. CPA responds by disclosing confidential tax information to defend herself.

requirements.question: Scenario 1 (not listing name) - Is this a Circular 230 violation?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Circular 230 §10.22 requires practitioner to sign returns they prepare. Omitting name to avoid scrutiny is a violation.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Scenario 2 (destroyed documents) - What must the CPA do?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

If CPA knows client destroyed documents, continued representation would assist fraud. Must withdraw. Cannot affirmatively assist IRS but must not participate in concealment.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Scenario 3 (prior year error) - CPA's obligation?
options: Advise client of error and consequences
Report to IRS if client refuses
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

CPA must advise client of error and consequences of not amending. Cannot file amended without client consent. Not required to report but may consider withdrawal from ongoing engagement.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Scenario 4 (contingent fee) - Is this permitted?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Circular 230 §10.27 prohibits contingent fees for preparing original tax returns. Allowed for amended returns, claims for refund, or IRS exam defense.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Must sign returns prepared
Cannot assist in fraud or concealment
No contingent fees for original returns

tbs.title: Corporate Charitable Contribution Limitation
tbs.scenario:
ABC Corporation (C corp, calendar year) has the following Year 1 data:

Gross profit: $2,800,000
Operating expenses: $1,500,000
Dividend income (20% owned domestic): $100,000
Capital gains: $150,000
Charitable contributions made: $180,000

ABC had no NOL or charitable carryforwards from prior years.
Charitable limitation: 10% of modified taxable income

requirements.question: Calculate taxable income before charitable deduction and DRD.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross profit $2,800,000 - Operating expenses $1,500,000 + Dividend income $100,000 + Capital gains $150,000 = $1,550,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the charitable contribution limitation.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Modified TI for charitable = TI before charitable deduction and DRD = $1,550,000. Limit = 10% × $1,550,000 = $155,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the charitable contribution deduction allowed.
requirements.explanation:

tbs.title: At-Risk Rules
tbs.scenario:
Tom invests in a real estate limited partnership. Year 1 activity:

Investment and financing:
• Cash contributed: $100,000
• Recourse loan (Tom personally liable): $150,000
• Nonrecourse loan (secured by property): $200,000

Year 1 results:
• Partnership allocation of loss: ($180,000)

Prior suspended losses: $0

Note: Real estate qualified nonrecourse financing can be included in at-risk amount.

requirements.question: Calculate Tom's at-risk amount.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

At-risk = Cash $100,000 + Recourse debt $150,000 + Qualified nonrecourse financing $200,000 = $450,000 (real estate exception allows qualified nonrecourse)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How much of the $180,000 loss is deductible under at-risk rules?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

At-risk amount $450,000 > Loss $180,000. Entire loss passes at-risk hurdle.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Tom's ending at-risk amount.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Beginning at-risk $450,000 - Loss allowed $180,000 = $270,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If this were equipment leasing (not real estate), what would be at-risk?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

For non-real estate activities, nonrecourse debt is NOT at-risk. At-risk would be Cash $100,000 + Recourse $150,000 = $250,000

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: At-risk includes cash and amounts personally liable for
Real estate exception allows qualified nonrecourse financing
Non-real estate: only cash and recourse debt count

tbs.title: IRS Penalties and Interest
tbs.scenario:
DataTech LLC filed its Year 1 partnership return (Form 1065) 4 months late. The partnership has 5 partners.

Additionally, partner Robert failed to pay his individual tax liability of $50,000 by April 15. He received a 90-day letter but did not respond. The deficiency was ultimately assessed:
• Amount owed: $50,000
• Months late for failure to pay: 8 months
• Months late for failure to file (if applicable): 0 (he filed on time)

Failure to pay penalty: 0.5% per month (max 25%)
Partnership late filing: $235 per partner per month (max 12 months)

requirements.question: Calculate partnership late filing penalty.
requirements.explanation:

$235 × 5 partners × 4 months = $4,700

requirements.question: Calculate Robert's failure to pay penalty.

requirements.question: What happens when Robert ignores the 90-day letter?

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Partnership penalty is per partner per month
Failure to pay: 0.5% per month, max 25%

tbs.title: Form 4797 - Sale of Business Property
tbs.scenario:
Martinez Manufacturing sold the following business assets during 2024:

Asset 1: Manufacturing Equipment
• Original cost: $180,000
• Accumulated depreciation (MACRS): $145,000
• Sales price: $95,000
• Date acquired: March 2019
• Date sold: August 2024

Asset 2: Delivery Truck
• Original cost: $55,000
• Accumulated depreciation: $55,000 (fully depreciated)
• Sales price: $8,000
• Date acquired: June 2018
• Date sold: May 2024

Asset 3: Factory Building
• Original cost: $800,000
• Accumulated depreciation (straight-line): $200,000
• Sales price: $950,000
• Land not included (reported separately)
• Date acquired: January 2010
• Date sold: November 2024

Assume no other §1231 transactions and no §1231 losses in the prior 5 years.

requirements.question: Calculate the total gain on Asset 1 (Manufacturing Equipment).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Adjusted basis = $180,000 - $145,000 = $35,000. Gain = $95,000 - $35,000 = $60,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What amount of Asset 1 gain is §1245 depreciation recapture (ordinary income)?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

§1245 recapture is the lesser of: (1) gain realized ($60,000) or (2) depreciation taken ($145,000). Since gain ($60,000) < depreciation ($145,000), entire gain is ordinary income.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the §1245 recapture (ordinary income) on Asset 2 (Delivery Truck).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Adjusted basis = $55,000 - $55,000 = $0. Gain = $8,000 - $0 = $8,000. Since gain ($8,000) < depreciation ($55,000), entire $8,000 gain is §1245 recapture.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the §1250 unrecaptured gain (25% rate) on Asset 3 (Building).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total gain = $950,000 - ($800,000 - $200,000) = $350,000. §1250 unrecaptured gain is limited to depreciation taken = $200,000 (taxed at max 25%). Remaining $150,000 is §1231 gain (LTCG rates).

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: After netting, how is the remaining $150,000 building gain characterized?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

The $150,000 excess gain ($350,000 total - $200,000 unrecaptured §1250) is §1231 gain, which is treated as long-term capital gain when §1231 gains exceed §1231 losses.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: §1245 recapture converts depreciation to ordinary income on personal property
§1250 unrecaptured gain applies to straight-line depreciation on real property (25% max rate)
§1231 netting determines final character (LTCG or ordinary)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total income: $180,000 + $85,000 + $3,200 + $8,500 + $15,000 - $4,000 = $287,700. Minus: 1/2 SE tax ($6,007) + IRA contribution $7,000 + Student loan interest $2,500 = $272,193 (approximately)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Should the Smiths itemize or take the standard deduction?
options: Either, as the amounts are equal
requirements.explanation:

Itemized deductions of $30,000 exceed the standard deduction of $27,700, so they should itemize.

requirements.question: Calculate the QBI deduction for Mary's self-employment income (assume no W-2 wage limitation applies).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

QBI = SE income $85,000 × 20% = $17,000 (subject to income phase-out thresholds and overall taxable income limit)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

hints: Self-employment tax calculation: Net SE × 92.35% × 15.3%
Deductible portion of SE tax = 1/2 of SE tax (above-the-line)
SALT is capped at $10,000 for MFJ
QBI deduction is 20% of qualified business income (subject to limitations)

tbs.title: S Corporation Shareholder Basis
tbs.scenario:
Sarah is the sole shareholder of TechStart Inc., an S corporation. On January 1, Year 1, Sarah's stock basis was $40,000 and her debt basis was $15,000 (loan she made to the corporation).

Year 1 S Corporation Activity (Schedule K-1 items):
• Ordinary business income: $75,000
• Section 179 deduction: $8,000
• Charitable contributions: $3,000
• Tax-exempt interest income: $2,000
• Cash distribution to Sarah: $60,000

Additional Information:
• Sarah did not make any additional capital contributions
• The corporation did not repay any of Sarah's loan

requirements.question: Calculate Sarah's increase to stock basis for income items.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ordinary income $75,000 + Tax-exempt income $2,000 = $77,000. All income items (including tax-exempt) increase basis.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Sarah's decrease to stock basis for deduction and distribution items.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Section 179 $8,000 + Charitable $3,000 + Distribution $60,000 = $71,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Sarah's stock basis at year-end.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Beginning $40,000 + Income $77,000 - Deductions $11,000 - Distribution $60,000 = $46,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is Sarah's debt basis at year-end?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Debt basis remains $15,000. No reduction needed since stock basis was sufficient to absorb all deductions and distributions.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If the corporation had a $90,000 distribution instead of $60,000, how would the excess be taxed?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

After reducing stock basis to zero, excess distributions are capital gain (assuming no accumulated E&P from prior C corp years). Distributions first reduce stock basis, then when exhausted, are taxed as capital gains.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Basis ordering: Income first, then distributions, then deductions/losses
Tax-exempt income increases basis (preserves tax-exempt treatment)
Distributions reduce stock basis (cannot go below zero)
Distribution excess (after zeroing stock basis) is capital gain
